section.title: Elihu's Third Speech
section.sectionRationale: Elihu's shortest speech targets a specific claim he attributes to Job, that righteousness profits a man nothing since God takes no notice of it either way. Elihu answers that human conduct, whether sin or righteousness, mainly affects other people, not God's own being, and that unanswered cries for help often stem from pride rather than genuine faith, since so few actually ask, 'Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?'
section.thesis: Elihu is right that God's greatness is not increased or diminished by any human's righteousness or sin, but he is too quick to conclude that Job's unanswered cries must therefore be the empty pleas of a proud man rather than the honest lament of a genuine sufferer.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Elihu's charge that Job claims righteousness profits nothing; his argument that human conduct mainly affects fellow humans, not God directly; his claim that many cries under oppression go unanswered because no one truly seeks God as Maker; and his conclusion that Job speaks in vain, multiplying words without knowledge.

units.label: More righteous than God?
units.note: what profit is there in not sinning

verses.text: And Elihu went on to say:
“Do you think this is just? You say, ‘I am more righteous than God.’
For you ask, ‘What does it profit me, and what benefit do I gain apart from sin?’
I will reply to you and to your friends as well.

units.label: Your righteousness reaches man, not God
units.note: look to the heavens and see

verses.text: Look to the heavens and see; gaze at the clouds high above you.
If you sin, what do you accomplish against Him? If you multiply your transgressions, what do you do to Him?
If you are righteous, what do you give Him, or what does He receive from your hand?
Your wickedness affects only a man like yourself, and your righteousness only a son of {{adam|man}}.

units.label: Songs in the night
units.note: no one asks, Where is God my Maker

verses.text: Men cry out under great oppression; they plead for relief from the arm of the mighty.
But no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives us {{zemirah|songs}} in the night,
who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the air?’
There they cry out, but He does not answer, because of the pride of evil men.
Surely God does not listen to {{shav|empty}} pleas, and the Almighty does not take note of it.
How much less, then, when you say that you do not see Him, that your case is before Him and you must wait for Him,
and further, that in His anger He has not punished or taken much notice of folly!
So Job opens his mouth in vain and multiplies words without knowledge.”

adam.gloss: Son of man, humanity in general. Elihu insists both sin and righteousness land on fellow human beings, ben adam, rather than adding to or subtracting from God's own being, an argument meant to humble Job's implied claim that his suffering somehow proves God's unfairness toward him personally.

zemirah.gloss: Song, melody. Elihu's poignant question, 'where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night,' pictures God as the source of comfort and even joy in the darkest hours, a gift Elihu says goes unsought by those who cry out only in pride rather than genuine faith.

shav.gloss: Emptiness, vanity, worthlessness. Elihu argues that God does not listen to shav, empty or hollow, pleas, cries that rise from wounded pride rather than humble trust, a claim he then applies, perhaps too quickly, to explain why Job feels unheard.

basic.context: Elihu answers a claim he attributes to Job, that there is no real benefit to righteousness since it does not seem to affect God's treatment of him one way or another. Elihu responds that human sin or righteousness mainly affects other humans, not God's own transcendent being, and that people often cry out to God from wounded pride rather than genuine faith, which is why, in Elihu's view, so many cries go unanswered.
basic.hermeneutics: Elihu's theological point about God's self sufficiency is sound and echoes Eliphaz's earlier argument in chapter 22; the risk, again, is applying a true general principle too confidently to Job's specific situation, effectively accusing Job of empty, prideful complaint rather than acknowledging that Job's confusion stems from a genuinely inexplicable providence.
basic.originalLanguages: Elihu insists that sin and righteousness reach only ben adam, one's fellow man, not God's own being. He pictures God as the one who gives zemirah, songs, in the night, a beautiful image of comfort largely unsought by people in their pride, and he concludes that shav, empty, pleas rightly go unanswered, a diagnosis he extends, not entirely fairly, to Job's own cries.
basic.typology: Elihu's picture of God as the giver of 'songs in the night' anticipates the fuller biblical pattern of God as comforter in darkness, ultimately embodied in Christ, who sang a hymn with His disciples on the night of His betrayal, Matthew 26:30, and who promises His presence in every dark hour.
basic.christ: Elihu asks whether anyone truly seeks 'God my Maker, who gives songs in the night'; Christ is the fullest answer to that question, the Maker who became flesh to dwell among sufferers, who sang a hymn on the very night He was betrayed, and who gives His people reason for songs even in their darkest nights, Acts 16:25, Psalm 42:8.
basic.god: God's greatness is not increased by human righteousness nor diminished by human sin, a point Scripture affirms elsewhere, Psalm 16:2, yet God is not indifferent to human cries; the whole book of Job, and especially its epilogue, shows a God who does hear, does answer, and does restore, even when the answer does not come on the timeline the sufferer expects.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 16:2 affirms that human goodness adds nothing to God's own being. Psalm 42:8 speaks of God's song being with the psalmist in the night. Acts 16:25 pictures Paul and Silas singing hymns in prison at midnight. Matthew 26:30 has Jesus sing a hymn with His disciples before His arrest.
basic.oneStory: Elihu rightly says God's greatness does not depend on human conduct, but his charge that Job's cries are merely proud and empty misjudges a genuine sufferer. The Bible's fuller answer to Elihu's own beautiful question, who gives songs in the night, arrives in Christ, who not only gives songs in dark hours but entered the darkest night of all so that sufferers would never cry out to an truly absent God.

context.literary: The shortest of Elihu's four speeches, structured as a direct rebuttal to a specific claim, that righteousness profits nothing, framed as Job's own words, though the exact phrase does not appear verbatim in Job's earlier speeches and may be Elihu's summary or paraphrase of Job's general complaint.
context.historical: The question of whether righteousness is worth pursuing if it brings no visible reward was a live concern in ancient wisdom traditions generally, reflected also in Malachi 3:14, where people ask what profit there is in keeping God's charge; Elihu's argument here participates in this wider wisdom conversation.
context.canonical: Elihu's claim that human conduct does not affect God's own being while still mattering enormously for human relationships and community anticipates later biblical reflection on divine transcendence and self sufficiency, Acts 17:25, Psalm 50:9-13.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct what Elihu perceives as a distorted view that righteousness is pointless since it does not visibly benefit the righteous person, while also, less successfully, diagnosing Job's unanswered cries as the empty pleas of pride rather than the honest lament of true suffering.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Elihu's claim about God's self sufficiency, that human righteousness or sin does not add to or subtract from God's own being, is a sound and prescriptive theological truth. His specific diagnosis that Job's cries are unanswered because of pride is Elihu's own fallible judgment about Job's heart, not a truth the narrative itself confirms.

debates.question: Does Elihu accurately represent Job's actual position when he says Job asked, 'what profit is there in not sinning,' 35:3?

views.label: A fair summary of Job's implied complaint
views.case: Job has repeatedly protested that his righteousness has brought him no protection from suffering, 9:22, 21:7, making Elihu's paraphrase a reasonable, if pointed, restatement.

views.label: An unfair caricature
views.case: Job has never said righteousness is worthless in itself; he has said that righteousness does not guarantee visible prosperity in this life, a more nuanced claim that Elihu's paraphrase flattens into something closer to nihilism.

debates.bearing: Even granting Elihu some fairness in summarizing Job's frustration, the difference between 'righteousness does not guarantee prosperity' and 'righteousness profits nothing' is significant, and Elihu's answer addresses the more extreme version of the claim rather than Job's actual, more careful position.

debates.question: Is Elihu correct that Job's cries go unanswered because of pride, 35:12-13?

views.label: A reasonable general principle, rightly applied
views.case: Scripture elsewhere warns that God resists the proud, James 4:6, and Elihu may simply be applying this true pattern to Job's specific case.

views.label: A premature and ultimately incorrect diagnosis of Job specifically
views.case: God's own epilogue verdict never accuses Job of prideful, empty pleading, and in fact commends Job's speech over the friends', 42:7-8; God does eventually answer Job directly, from the whirlwind, suggesting Elihu's specific charge against Job was mistaken even if the general principle about pride has some truth to it.

debates.bearing: The general principle about pride hindering prayer is sound biblical teaching, but its specific application to Job appears to be another instance of Elihu, like the friends before him, being right in general and wrong in the particular case at hand.

study.originalLanguages: Elihu insists sin and righteousness reach only ben adam, fellow humanity, not God's own transcendent being. He asks why so few seek 'God my Maker, who gives zemirah, songs, in the night,' and concludes that shav, empty or worthless, pleas rightly go unheard, a diagnosis he applies to Job's continued protest, calling it words multiplied 'without knowledge.'

typology.type: God as the giver of songs in the night, 35:10
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who sang a hymn with His disciples on the night of His betrayal and gives His people reason to sing even in darkness, Matthew 26:30, Acts 16:25.

study.christ: Elihu's beautiful, half rhetorical question, 'where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night,' finds its fullest answer in Jesus Christ, God made flesh, who entered the deepest darkness of all, betrayal and the cross, singing a hymn with His disciples the very night He was handed over, Matthew 26:30, and who now gives His people songs of praise even in their own darkest nights, as Paul and Silas sang hymns in a midnight prison cell, Acts 16:25.
study.god: God's own being is not enriched by human righteousness or impoverished by human sin, a truth the whole Bible affirms, Psalm 50:9-13, Acts 17:25; yet this same God, this book insists elsewhere, does hear the cries of the afflicted and does act on their behalf, so Elihu's true premise should not be pressed into the false conclusion that God is simply unmoved by human suffering.

crossReferences.note: Affirms that human goodness adds nothing to God's own being, echoing Elihu's point about divine self sufficiency.
God is not served by human hands, as though He needed anything, since He Himself gives life and breath to all things.
By day the LORD directs His love, at night His song is with me, the very comfort Elihu says goes unsought.
Jesus sings a hymn with His disciples on the night of His betrayal, embodying the God who gives songs in the night.

study.oneStory: Elihu rightly insists God's greatness does not rise or fall with human conduct, yet his diagnosis that Job's cries are merely proud and empty misreads a genuine sufferer's honest lament. The Bible answers Elihu's own beautiful question, who gives songs in the night, with Christ, who entered the darkest night in history and now gives His people real songs of hope in every dark season since.

sermon.title: Job: Rebuked in Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon on Job 32:1-37:24 treats Elihu's speeches as a unified rebuke and preparation for God's own answer, engaging directly with this speech's argument that God's justice and self sufficiency mean Job's complaints rest on a misunderstanding rather than genuine grievance.

quiz.q: What claim does Elihu attribute to Job at the start of chapter 35?

options.text: That righteousness profits a man nothing, since sin or virtue seems to make no difference to how God treats him.
options.note: Verses 2-3 frame this as the specific complaint Elihu is answering.

options.text: That Job wants to abandon belief in God entirely.
options.note: Job's complaint concerns the apparent lack of visible reward for righteousness, not abandoning belief in God.

options.text: That Job believes his friends are entirely right about him.
options.note: Job has consistently opposed his friends' theology, not agreed with it.

options.text: That Job wants to worship other gods.
options.note: No such claim appears anywhere in the text.

quiz.q: What is Elihu's main argument in 35:5-8?

options.text: Human sin or righteousness mainly affects other people, not God's own transcendent being.
options.note: Verses 6-8 state this directly, that neither adds to nor takes from what God is in Himself.

options.text: God is completely indifferent to human behavior.
options.note: Elihu's point is about God's self sufficiency, not indifference; the rest of the book shows God deeply engaged with human conduct.

options.text: Only the wicked affect other people; the righteous affect no one.
options.note: Elihu says both righteousness and wickedness affect fellow humans, not that only wickedness does.

options.text: Human behavior directly changes God's essential nature.
options.note: Elihu argues precisely the opposite, that God's own being is unaffected either way.

quiz.q: According to Elihu, why do many cries under oppression go unanswered, 35:9-13?

options.text: Because people cry out from pride rather than genuine trust, seeking relief without truly seeking God as Maker.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly attributes unanswered cries to 'the pride of evil men.'

options.text: Because God is powerless to help the oppressed.
options.note: Elihu's whole speech assumes God's active power and sovereignty; powerlessness is not his claim.

options.text: Because oppression does not actually exist in the world.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly acknowledges real oppression and real cries for help.

options.text: Because God only listens to wealthy people.
options.note: Elihu never makes a claim about wealth determining whose prayers are heard.

quiz.q: How does Elihu's question, 'where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night,' 35:10, ultimately get answered in the Bible's larger story?

options.text: In Christ, who entered the darkest night of betrayal singing a hymn, and who gives His people songs of hope in their own darkness.
options.note: Matthew 26:30 and Acts 16:25 both show this pattern of God's presence and song even amid suffering.

options.text: The Bible never returns to this question again.
options.note: The theme of God's presence and comfort in the night recurs throughout Scripture, from Psalm 42:8 to the Gospels.

options.text: By teaching that God never gives comfort during suffering.
options.note: The pattern across Scripture is the opposite, God's comforting presence precisely in dark seasons.

options.text: By proving that no one can ever know God as Maker.
options.note: Elihu's question is rhetorical, lamenting that few seek this knowledge, not declaring it impossible.

quiz.q: How should Elihu's true premise about God's self sufficiency be weighed against his specific charge against Job?

options.text: The premise about God's transcendence is sound, but applying it to conclude Job's cries are empty and prideful goes beyond what the book itself confirms.
options.note: God's epilogue verdict, 42:7-8, favors Job's honesty over the confident, harsh diagnoses of both the friends and, in places, Elihu.

options.text: Both the premise and the specific charge against Job are fully endorsed by the book's ending.
options.note: The book's ending vindicates Job's speech over his accusers, not Elihu's specific charge of empty pride.

options.text: Neither the premise nor the charge has any value.
options.note: The premise about God's self sufficiency is sound theology, affirmed elsewhere in Scripture, even if the specific application to Job overreaches.

options.text: The premise is false, but the charge against Job is entirely correct.
options.note: The premise about divine self sufficiency is true; it is the specific application to Job that appears overreaching.

questions: Elihu argues that our righteousness or sin mainly affects other people, not God's own being. How does remembering this shape the way you think about the purpose of living righteously?
Elihu asks why so few seek 'God my Maker, who gives songs in the night.' Have you experienced God giving you an unexpected song, comfort, or hope in a genuinely dark season?
Elihu diagnosed Job's cries as empty pride, but God later vindicated Job's honesty. How can you tell the difference between prideful complaint and honest lament in your own prayers?
Christ sang a hymn with His disciples the very night He was betrayed. What would it look like for you to choose worship or gratitude in the middle of your own hardest night?

Elihu's true theology was applied to Job too quickly and too harshly. When you want to offer someone spiritual truth in their suffering, how can you first make sure you have truly listened to their actual pain?

translationNotes.note: 'What benefit do I gain apart from sin' is Elihu's paraphrase of Job's implied complaint; the exact phrase does not appear verbatim earlier in Job's speeches, suggesting Elihu may be summarizing rather than quoting directly.
'Songs in the night' likely refers to songs of praise or comfort given even amid affliction, a phrase with resonance in Psalm 42:8 and later Christian hymnody about finding joy in suffering.
'Empty pleas' translates shav, which can also mean vanity or falsehood; Elihu's point is that hollow, self focused cries, rather than all prayer, fail to receive a hearing.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 35:1-16. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
